does: This paper demonstrates that mechanism design alone cannot ensure optimal cooperation between AI agents when contracts are incomplete—that is, when they cannot account for all possible future contingencies. The authors formally prove using incomplete contract theory that such informational gaps inevitably create welfare losses that no realistically designed mechanism can fully eliminate. They then show experimentally that LLM-based agents augmented with prosocial preferences, which value others' welfare alongside their own, can overcome this limitation and achieve superior social welfare outcomes in multi-agent resource-allocation tasks and social dilemmas without sacrificing individual benefits.
connects: This work builds on mechanism design theory, which has long aimed to align individual incentives with collective welfare through rule design, but acknowledges that it operates under idealized assumptions of complete contracting. The paper draws from incomplete contract theory in economics to formalize when and why these mechanisms fail. It responds to the practical challenge of AI safety in multi-agent settings, connecting to prior work on cooperative game theory, social dilemmas in AI, and recent efforts to instill human-aligned values in language models. The experimental validation using LLM agents situates this within the emerging literature on agent coordination and the use of large language models as decision-making entities in interactive environments.
enables: This work opens new directions for AI safety by suggesting that mechanism design must be complemented with explicit value alignment through prosocial objectives. It points toward future research on how to design and train AI agents that genuinely internalize others' welfare as part of their objective function, rather than merely responding to external incentives. The findings could inform the development of more robust cooperative AI systems, multiagent reinforcement learning with intrinsic prosocial components, and safety strategies that combine institutional design with value engineering. It also raises questions about how to verify and implement prosocial preferences in large language models at scale and whether such approaches generalize beyond controlled experimental settings to real-world AI deployment scenarios.
